source: Learning bash with Rocky - Using Variables: Deleting/locking variables, environment variables, export, and command substitution

questions.q: Which command locks a variable so it cannot be changed?
questions.why: readonly/typeset -r makes a variable read-only; reassigning or unsetting it then errors.

questions.q: What does `set -u` at the start of a script do?
options: Stops execution if an undeclared (unset) variable is used
questions.why: set -u aborts the script when an undeclared variable is referenced.

questions.q: Which command displays all the environment variables?
questions.why: env shows the environment variables; set shows all shell/system variables.

questions.q: Which special variable holds the return code of the last command executed?
questions.why: $? is the last command's exit code; $$ is the script's process id.

questions.q: Which special variable holds the process id of the running script?
questions.why: $$ is the PID of the script execution.

questions.q: What does the `export` command do to a variable?
options: Makes it available to the script's child processes
questions.why: export puts the variable in the environment so child processes see it (changes in a child do not flow back to the parent).

questions.q: What is the preferred syntax to store a command's output in a variable?
questions.why: $(command) is preferred over backticks, e.g. homedir=$(pwd).
